era_context: The early 4th century BCE. The Corinthian War (395–387 BCE) is in its mid-phase. Athens and Sparta are locked in strategic competition. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II is at its height. Rome is consolidating power in Latium and facing pressure from Gallic tribes. In China, the Warring States period is intensifying.

events.title: Corinthian War — continued military operations
events.description: The Corinthian War continued with campaigns in central Greece and the Peloponnese. The coalition of Athens, Corinth, Thebes, and Argos, backed by Persian subsidies, maintained pressure on Spartan hegemony. Agesilaus II led Spartan forces in defensive and offensive operations. The war remained inconclusive, with neither side able to achieve decisive victory. Persian diplomacy continued to manipulate the conflict to prevent any single Greek power from dominating.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon provides accounts of the Corinthian War, but his chronology for individual years is sometimes imprecise.
cross_references: Part of the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 393 BCE is sparse. Xenophon's Hellenica is the primary source, but it does not provide year-by-year detail for all campaigns. The historical record is heavily skewed toward Greek sources and reflects Greek interests. Roman, Asian, and African sources are minimal or absent.

graph_edges.note: The Corinthian War campaigns of 393 BCE were part of the broader conflict that concluded with the Peace of Antalcidas.
